one_line_takeaway: Grafting rotation- and scale-equivariant convolutions into a CNN improves adversarial robustness without adversarial training — a parallel, concatenation-fused rotation+scale design wins on FGSM/PGD — but the gains are demonstrated only on small CIFAR-scale data against non-adaptive attacks, and scale-equivariance trades away natural-corruption robustness.
key_finding: Embedding equivariant convolutions improves adversarial accuracy over the standard CNN WITHOUT adversarial training, but the winner depends on the threat. Against FGSM/PGD, the parallel design combining rotation+scale branches is consistently most robust across all epsilon at both depths; concatenation fusion beats weighted fusion; the cascaded and weighted-parallel variants are weak (10-layer: adversarial accuracy drops below 15% for FGSM and near-zero for PGD at higher epsilon). A fully-equivariant 10-layer model (every conv layer equivariant, no standard branch) reaches 73.01% FGSM and 64.96% PGD accuracy at epsilon=0.01 on CIFAR-10, supporting the claim that end-to-end symmetry compounds the benefit. Importantly, on NATURAL corruptions (CIFAR-10C) the plain rotation-only Parallel GCNN is best (mean 13.59% at level 0.01 vs baseline 7.28%), while the rotation+scale variant actually UNDERPERFORMS the baseline on corruption (2.95% vs 7.28%) — so scale-equivariance helps adversarial but hurts natural-corruption robustness, a genuine trade-off the paper reports.
core_contribution: The paper studies whether ARCHITECTURAL symmetry priors alone (group-equivariant convolutions), without any adversarial training or data augmentation, can improve adversarial robustness of CNNs. Its own contributions are three: (1) a theoretical analysis arguing that rotation-equivariant layers make the margin function's gradient norm invariant across a group orbit (Theorem 1), suppress off-orbit gradient variation via orbit-averaging (Theorem 2), and therefore yield tighter CLEVER-certified robustness radii; (2) two concrete architectures that graft standard, rotation-equivariant (discrete P4 group), and scale-equivariant convolutions together — a 'parallel' design (independent branches fused by concatenation or weighted sum) and a 'cascaded' design (equivariant ops applied sequentially); (3) an empirical study across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and CIFAR-10C showing the parallel design with combined rotation+scale branches gives the best adversarial accuracy. Note: G-CNNs, Harmonic Nets, scale-steerable nets, FGSM, PGD, randomized smoothing, CLEVER, and feature denoising are all CITED prior work (Related Works / Definitions), not this paper's inventions — the paper's own novelty is applying equivariance specifically as an adversarial defense and the parallel/cascaded fusion designs.
threat_model: Inference-time evasion attacks: an adversary adds a norm-bounded, imperceptible perturbation delta (||delta||_p <= epsilon) to a test image to flip the classifier's prediction. The paper evaluates white-box gradient-based attacks — FGSM (single-step) and PGD (iterative, the stronger benchmark) — sweeping epsilon from 0.01 to 0.15. The defense is purely architectural (equivariant inductive bias); crucially the models are NOT adversarially trained, so the defense is proactive/design-time rather than reactive. CLEVER is used as an attack-agnostic certified lower bound on the perturbation radius. The paper also tests natural (non-adversarial) corruption robustness via CIFAR-10C.
limitations: Stated by the authors (Appendix B) and evident from results: (1) theory relies on local Lipschitz / CLEVER bounds only, which do not capture margin distributions or global adversarial risk, and the orbit-invariance guarantee holds strictly only under orthogonality assumptions that SCALE transformations violate (scale robustness is argued only heuristically via 'gradient smoothing'). (2) Evaluation is confined to small 32x32 datasets (CIFAR-10/100/10C) and standard l_p FGSM/PGD; no ImageNet-scale, no adaptive/attack-aware adversaries — a real concern given CLEVER-based/gradient-smoothing defenses have historically been vulnerable to obfuscated-gradient critiques (the paper even cites Athalye et al.). (3) Equivariant layers are inserted only at early network stages and the compute overhead of group convolutions is unquantified. (4) Absolute robust accuracies collapse toward 0% at larger epsilon and on CIFAR-100, and the promised head-to-head vs a PGD-adversarially-trained CNN is deferred to Appendix E (not in the main tables). (5) The rotation+scale model's degradation on CIFAR-10C shows the architectural prior is not a free lunch.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10C (19 natural corruption types, 5 severity levels; used at perturbation levels 0.01/0.02/0.03/0.04)
quantitative_results: Fully-equivariant 10-layer model: 73.01% FGSM accuracy and 64.96% PGD accuracy at epsilon=0.01 on CIFAR-10 (no adversarial training)
CIFAR-10C mean accuracy at perturbation level 0.01: Parallel GCNN 13.59% (best) vs Baseline CNN 7.28% vs Parallel GCNN Rot&Scale 2.95% vs Cascaded GCNN 2.76% vs Weighted GCNN 3.01%
CIFAR-10C Parallel GCNN mean accuracy degrades to 3.28% / 0.81% / 0.23% at levels 0.02 / 0.03 / 0.04 (all models fall toward 0% at level 0.04)
Best single CIFAR-10C cell: Parallel GCNN on Saturate = 22.09% at level 0.01
10-layer Cascaded and Weighted-Parallel GCNNs: adversarial accuracy < 15% under FGSM and near-zero under PGD at higher epsilon
Adversarial epsilon swept from 0.01 to 0.15; all results averaged over 5 random seeds with +/-1 std
baselines_compared: Parallel GCNN with Rotation- and Scale-Equivariant branches
Weighted Parallel GCNN (learnable fusion weights instead of concatenation)
Fully-equivariant model (all layers equivariant, no standard branch)
Standard CNN with PGD adversarial training (comparison deferred to Appendix E)
